title: Lawful Bases in the Wild
intro: Recognizing personal data was lesson one, Agent. Failing to anonymize it was lesson two. Now the question that governs everything: by what RIGHT do you process it? The law grants six lawful bases. Most villagers believe the answer is always "consent." Most villagers are wrong. Judge each case.
referenceStrip: THE SIX: CONSENT · CONTRACT · LEGAL OBLIGATION · VITAL INTERESTS · PUBLIC TASK · LEGITIMATE INTERESTS

cards.item: Odile records which patrons owe silver on their tab.
explain: The classic over-use. Consent could be withdrawn at any moment — and a debt cannot simply stop being owed because someone changed their mind about being tracked.
Running a tab IS an agreement; recording the debt is necessary to perform it. Asking consent here would be absurd — and worse, consent can be withdrawn. Debts cannot.
Unnecessary when a contract fits squarely — legitimate interests is for cases the other five bases don't cleanly cover, not a shortcut around them.

cards.item: Bram logs every entrant at the gates, as the Council's Watch Decree commands.
explain: A binding decree leaves Bram no choice — that is the basis, and its scope is set by the decree itself, not by Bram's preferences.
There is no meaningful choice here; a decree is not a request. Consent obtained under compulsion isn't lawful consent at all.
No agreement exists between Bram and the travelers he logs — only a duty imposed on Bram by the Council.

cards.item: A healer treats an unconscious traveler and records his condition.
explain: He cannot consent — he is unconscious. Reaching for this basis anyway is exactly the reflex this lesson exists to break.
He cannot consent; his life is at stake. Vital interests exists precisely for this — and only while the incapacity does.
The healer is not exercising an official function of the village; this is emergency care, not administration.

cards.item: The Council conducts the village census.
explain: An official function laid down in the village's law. Authorities acting as authorities generally cannot lean on legitimate interests — their basis is the task itself.
Authorities acting as authorities generally cannot lean on legitimate interests — that escape hatch is for everyone else, not for the Council performing its own duties.
A census does not wait on individual willingness — it is compulsory by nature, which rules consent out.

cards.item: The Division keeps a watchlist of suspected Shadownet infiltrators at the festival.
explain: Security is a legitimate interest — but this basis is a BALANCING TEST, not a blank warrant: necessity, proportionality, and the suspects' rights weigh against it, and it must survive challenge. The only basis that argues for its life every time it is used.
No suspect is going to consent to being watchlisted — and a basis that only works when the subject agrees isn't fit for this purpose.
No agreement exists between the Division and the people on its list — quite the opposite.

cards.item: Fennick wants to feed his old sales ledgers to the judgment engine to train its pricing.
choices: He already has the data — no basis needed
A new purpose needs its own basis AND a compatibility test
explain: Legitimate interests might eventually apply — but only after the compatibility question is asked, not as a shortcut around it.
The trap most merchants fall into. Holding data lawfully for selling apples does not license training an oracle with it.
Purpose limitation again, Agent — every NEW purpose must find its own lawful basis and pass compatibility with the original one.

cards.item: A portrait-painter sells sketches of recognizable festival-goers to a broadsheet.
explain: Commercial exploitation of identifiable likenesses, no contract, no duty, no emergency — the balancing test collapses against him. Here, and not everywhere, consent truly is the answer.
The balancing test collapses against him: a stranger's face sold for profit, with no relationship or necessity to lean on.
The painter holds no official function whatsoever — he is a private merchant, not the Council.

cards.item: The Millers' Guild records members' ailments to assign lighter duties.
choices: Explicit consent — this is special category data
explain: Ordinary consent is not enough here — health data is SPECIAL CATEGORY data, which needs a stricter Article 9 condition on top of an ordinary basis, not instead of one.

Health data is SPECIAL CATEGORY — the ordinary six are not enough; an Article 9 condition must ALSO be met, explicit consent being the usual one here. Remember the faction-banner scroll: some data carries double locks.
Guild membership is a relationship, but membership alone doesn't license collecting health data — special category data needs its own, stricter condition regardless of what other basis might apply.
